perturbation.original_text: VIP shall indemnify, defend and hold BNL and its officers, directors, stockholders, employees, agents and representatives harmless from any and all claims, actions, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys' fees and expenses, arising out of any third party claims of infringement of any United States patents, or a trade secret, or any copyright, trademark, service mark, trade name or similar proprietary rights alleged to have occurred related to VIP's providing of EDP Services pursuant to this Agreement or relating to the VIP System used in providing such EDP Services.
perturbation.changed_text: VIP shall indemnify, defend and hold BNL and its officers, directors, stockholders, employees, agents and representatives harmless from third party claims of infringement of any United States patents alleged to have occurred related to VIP's providing of EDP Services pursuant to this Agreement or relating to the VIP System used in providing such EDP Services. BNL is responsible for all expenses, including attorney's fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: By removing coverage for trade secrets, copyrights, trademarks, service marks, and trade names, and making BNL responsible for all expenses, including attorney's fees, the contract reduces the protection provided to BNL in case of intellectual property infringement. This introduces potential legal risk, particularly regarding copyright claims. The added sentence explicitly makes BNL responsible for expenses, including attorney's fees and costs, which weakens the indemnification clause, potentially contradicting implied warranty laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 U.S. Code § 504 - Remedies for infringement: Damages and profits
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text limits VIP's liability regarding copyright infringement claims, while 17 U.S. Code § 504 outlines the remedies available to a copyright holder for infringement. By reducing VIP's indemnification obligations, the contract could force BNL to bear costs associated with copyright infringement, contradicting the protections afforded by copyright law. Moreover, the original text included attorney's fees and expenses in the indemnification. The omission of 'damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys' fees and expenses' is a direct contradiction, since the law mandates that such expenses can be awarded to the copyright holder.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 504 - Remedies for infringement: Damages and profits | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 504 - Remedies for infringement: Damages and profits U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In General.— Except as otherwise provided by this title, an infringer of copyright is liable for either— (1) the copyright owner’s actual damages and any additional profits of the infringer, as provided by subsection (b); or (2) statutory damages, as provided by subsection (c). (b) Actual Damages and Profits.— The copyright owner is entitled to recover the actual damages suffered by him or her as a result of the infringement, and any profits of the infringer that are attributable to the infringement and are not taken into account in computing the actual damages. In establishing the infringer’s profits, the copyright owner is required to present proof only of the infringer’s gross revenue, and the infringer is required to prove his or her deductible expenses and the elements of profit attributable to factors other than the copyrighted work. (c) Statutory Damages.— (1) Except as provided by clause (2) of this subsection, the copyright owner may elect, at any time before final judgment is rendered, to recover, instead of actual damages and profits, an award of statutory damages for all infringements involved in the action, with respect to any one work, for which any one infringer is liable individually, or for which any two or more infringers are liable jointly and severally, in a sum of not less than $750 or more than $30,000 as the court considers just. For the purposes of this subsection, all the parts of a compilation or derivative work constitute one work. (2) In a case where the copyright owner sustains the burden of proving, and the court finds, that infringement was committed willfully, the court in its discretion may increase the award of statutory damages to a sum o
perturbation.justification: The original text provides broad indemnification to BNL, covering various intellectual property claims and associated expenses. The changed text significantly narrows this indemnification by removing coverage for trade secrets, copyrights, trademarks, service marks, and trade names, and shifting the responsibility for all expenses, including attorney's fees and costs, to BNL. This directly contradicts the protections afforded by copyright law, as highlighted in 17 U.S. Code § 504, which outlines remedies for infringement, including damages and profits. By omitting coverage for these damages and expenses, the modified contract potentially forces BNL to bear costs that VIP would have originally covered, creating a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Maintain property insurance in an amount sufficient to replace or reconstruct the hardware, software, data and facilities necessary for VIP to operate the VIP System and otherwise provide the EDP Services set forth in this Agreement and, upon written request, shall provide BNL with evidence of the coverage, including all applicable limits and conditions, and, upon written request, shall provide BNL with evidence of all renewals, cancellations, expirations or modifications of the coverage
perturbation.changed_text: Maintain property insurance related to VIP's operation of the VIP System. Evidence of coverage may be provided to BNL at VIP's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'sufficient amount', the clause becomes vague and allows VIP to carry minimal insurance that would not adequately cover BNL's potential losses. Removing 'all applicable limits and conditions' weakens BNL's ability to assess the adequacy of the insurance. This introduces risk, contradicting potential requirements for adequate insurance coverage to protect business partners.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arkansas Code § 23-60-104 requires an insurable interest for insurance policies, implying that the coverage should be adequate to cover the potential loss. The modified clause's vague language about maintaining 'property insurance related to VIP's operation' and removing the details about coverage amount creates ambiguity about whether the insurance adequately protects BNL's interests. By reducing the detail of coverage, it is possible the policy won't adequately protect BNL, or that BNL cannot assess whether it does. Moreover, the insurance company domiciled in Arkansas, thus its actions are influenced by Arkansas law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes essential information about the sufficiency of insurance coverage and the details of the coverage provided, potentially creating a legal loophole. The original contract specifies maintaining 'property insurance in an amount sufficient to replace or reconstruct the hardware, software, data and facilities necessary for VIP'. The altered contract only requires maintaining 'property insurance related to VIP's operation of the VIP System', a vague requirement. Because the Scraped Law Snippets do not provide information applicable to this situation, the basis for a contradiction is made on the other information provided.

perturbation.original_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement, or its breach, or its validity or interpretation, except claims for injunctive relief and claims involving necessary third parties who refuse to participate, shall be settled by binding arbitration in accordance with the then current Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association (" AAA") subject, however, to the following: 1. The location for the arbitration shall be at such location reasonably designated by the arbitrators. 2. Such arbitration shall be heard and determined by a panel of three (3) arbitrators in accordance with the then current rules or regulations of the AAA relating to commercial disputes. One arbitrator shall be appointed by each party to serve on the panel. One neutral arbitrator shall be appointed by the AAA and shall serve as chairperson of the three arbitrator panel. Such neutral arbitrator shall be an attorney with experience in handling disputes relating to the providing of out sourced electronic data processing services. 3. The arbitration award shall be binding on the parties and may be enforced in any court of competent jurisdiction. 4. The prevailing party in such arbitration shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs incurred in such arbitration proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement, or its breach, or its validity or interpretation, except claims for injunctive relief, shall be settled by binding arbitration. The location for the arbitration shall be determined solely by VIP. The arbitration award shall be final. Each party shall bear its own costs, including attorney's fees.
perturbation.explanation: This modification omits the requirement for the arbitration to follow the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the AAA, allowing for potentially unfair arbitration procedures. It removes the panel of three arbitrators, and allows VIP to unilaterally determine the arbitration location. Finally, it removes the provision that the prevailing party is entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs, which can deter valid claims. These changes create a system that is highly favorable to VIP and potentially unfair to BNL.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S. Code § 10 outlines the conditions under which a court can vacate an arbitration award, including corruption, fraud, undue means, or evident partiality of the arbitrators. By removing the requirement to follow the AAA rules and giving VIP sole discretion over the arbitration location, the contract increases the risk that the arbitration process could be conducted unfairly, potentially leading to a result that could be challenged under 9 U.S. Code § 10. Furthermore, by stating that the arbitration award shall be final, it attempts to circumvent the possibility of vacating, something which is prohibited by law. Finally, the lack of a cost-shifting provision (prevailing party recovers fees) creates a disincentive to bringing legitimate claims since the claimant must bear all legal costs, regardless of success. This introduces a contradiction with the principle of fair and equitable dispute resolution, implicitly protected by law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes key provisions that ensure fair arbitration, potentially violating the spirit of fair dispute resolution implicitly upheld by law and increasing risks outlined in 9 U.S. Code § 10. By omitting the requirement to follow AAA rules and granting VIP sole discretion over the arbitration location, the amended contract raises concerns about impartiality and fairness. As Scraped Law Snippet 2 indicates, 9 U.S. Code § 10 allows a court to vacate an arbitration award if it was procured by 'corruption, fraud, or undue means' or if there was 'evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators'. The original contract had procedural safeguards, but the modified version lacks these, thus creating a contradiction.
